briefing.headline: 6 high-priority items in the latest EM scan
briefing.summary: Top sources today: European Journal of Emergency Medicine, REBEL EM, The Bottom Line. Main themes: trials, point-of-care ultrasound, reviews. Best early reads: Association between patient-to-nurse ratios in emergency departments and patient, staff, and organizational outcomes: a systematic review; HI-PEITHO Trial: Ultrasound-facilitated, Catheter Directed Fibrinolysis for PE.
briefing.editorialSummary:
Today’s set is strongest when it turns new capability into disciplined bedside use rather than procedural enthusiasm. The clearest practical wins are operational and ultrasound-heavy: emergency physicians diagnosing DVT accurately and far faster at the bedside, serratus anterior plane block looking increasingly ready for frontline rib-fracture analgesia, and TWIST plus POCUS giving paediatric torsion assessment more structure without making it safe to rule torsion out.

The PE cluster is the real editorial hinge. HI-PEITHO suggests a limited benefit for catheter-directed fibrinolysis in selected intermediate-risk patients, but the paired commentary and First10EM critique both argue for a much higher threshold before treating technical availability as routine indication. That same caution runs through the rest of the list: imported head-injury pathways may save resources but can carry safety costs, postoperative transfusion thresholds still should not drift liberal by reflex, and trial-design papers in sepsis remain more useful for building better studies than for changing bedside fluid practice.

A few quieter but sticky points are worth carrying into shift: paediatric ketamine safety still benefits from better pooled estimates rather than new dogma, preceding OHCA alone should not end mechanical-circulatory-support conversations, and ED staffing debates still need stronger department-specific evidence than people often claim. Overall, this is a reading set about sharper tools and stricter thresholds.

dailyEditorial.title: Association between patient-to-nurse ratios in emergency departments and patient, staff, and organizational outcomes: a systematic review
dailyEditorial.takeaway: Use this as support for acuity-aware staffing advocacy, not as proof that one fixed ratio solves ED harm. Boarding load, task mix and local workflow still matter more than any headline number.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This review addresses a question every crowded department feels but rarely answers cleanly: whether explicit ED nurse staffing ratios improve meaningful outcomes.
dailyEditorial.summary: This review addresses a question every crowded department feels but rarely answers cleanly: whether explicit ED nurse staffing ratios improve meaningful outcomes. The available summary is much stronger on methods than results, so its practical value is less about proving one correct ratio and more about showing that staffing arguments still need better ED-specific evidence.

dailyEditorial.title: HI-PEITHO Trial: Ultrasound-facilitated, Catheter Directed Fibrinolysis for PE
dailyEditorial.takeaway: Start with good anticoagulation and honest risk stratification. Reserve catheter-directed escalation for the subset with credible deterioration risk and a team that can deliver it well.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: HI-PEITHO keeps intermediate-risk PE interesting without making anticoagulation any less central.
dailyEditorial.summary: HI-PEITHO keeps intermediate-risk PE interesting without making anticoagulation any less central. The signal for reduced short-term decompensation appears real in a selected subgroup, but the benefit is narrower than procedural enthusiasm can make it sound.

dailyEditorial.title: TOP: Liberal or restrictive post-op transfusion
dailyEditorial.takeaway: Do not let cardiac comorbidity automatically push you to a liberal threshold. Individualise for ischemia and bleeding, but restrictive practice still looks like the default position to defend.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: TOP is useful because it revisits transfusion thresholds in the patients who make clinicians most uneasy: postoperative adults with cardiac risk.
dailyEditorial.summary: TOP is useful because it revisits transfusion thresholds in the patients who make clinicians most uneasy: postoperative adults with cardiac risk. The practical lesson is restraint against transfusing to comfort rather than to clear physiologic need.

dailyEditorial.title: Serratus Anterior Plane Block for Acute Rib Fractures in the Emergency Department: A Randomized Controlled Trial
dailyEditorial.takeaway: If your team already has ultrasound-guided regional anaesthesia capability, SAPB looks increasingly like a practical ED tool. The question is now training and workflow more than whether the block works.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This randomized trial pushes regional analgesia closer to mainstream ED rib-fracture care.
dailyEditorial.summary: This randomized trial pushes regional analgesia closer to mainstream ED rib-fracture care. SAPB improved pain scores and was performed safely by emergency physicians, which matters because implementation has usually been the real barrier rather than proof of concept.

dailyEditorial.title: Catheter-Directed Fibrinolysis for Pulmonary Embolism: Is It Old NEWS?
dailyEditorial.takeaway: Read this alongside HI-PEITHO, not instead of it. Its main value is sharpening thresholds for escalation and resisting the slide from promising procedure to overused procedure.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This commentary is valuable because it checks procedural enthusiasm against patient selection and real-world applicability.
dailyEditorial.summary: This commentary is valuable because it checks procedural enthusiasm against patient selection and real-world applicability. The technique may be elegant, but the harder bedside question is still who is sick enough to benefit without being overtreated.

dailyEditorial.title: Modified brain injury guidelines in a regional Australian setting indicate significant resource savings but also safety concerns
dailyEditorial.takeaway: Severity-stratified TBI pathways can save work, but they need local validation before broad adoption. Resource efficiency is only a win if the missed-injury risk stays acceptably low.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: The Australian mBIG experience is appealing because it promises fewer neurosurgical consults, repeat scans and transfers in mostly mild head injury.
dailyEditorial.summary: The Australian mBIG experience is appealing because it promises fewer neurosurgical consults, repeat scans and transfers in mostly mild head injury. The catch is the important part: imported pathways may save resources while introducing safety concerns once they meet a different local system.

dailyEditorial.title: HI-PEITHO: More negative data on invasive therapy for higher risk PEs
dailyEditorial.takeaway: If you are tempted by invasive PE escalation, make sure you can explain the patient-centred benefit rather than just the composite endpoint. An anticoagulation-first, watchful approach still deserves a high bar to abandon.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: First10EM plays the useful contrarian role here by arguing that the apparent HI-PEITHO win may rest more on composite-endpoint design than on a clinically persuasive patient benefit.
dailyEditorial.summary: First10EM plays the useful contrarian role here by arguing that the apparent HI-PEITHO win may rest more on composite-endpoint design than on a clinically persuasive patient benefit. That scepticism matters because intermediate-risk PE is exactly where invasive therapy can spread on thin evidence.

dailyEditorial.title: Point-of-care ultrasound by trained emergency physicians versus radiologists for deep vein thrombosis diagnosis: a prospective blinded study on diagnostic accuracy and time efficiency
dailyEditorial.takeaway: If your department has trained operators, compression ultrasound for suspected DVT looks ready for wider frontline use. Faster diagnosis is the obvious win; the next question is how much downstream flow improves.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This is one of the sharper operationally relevant reads of the set.
dailyEditorial.summary: This is one of the sharper operationally relevant reads of the set. Trained emergency physicians matched radiology closely for accuracy while cutting time to diagnosis from hours to about one, which is exactly the kind of workflow gain that matters in a crowded ED.

dailyEditorial.title: Design Refinements for Sepsis Albumin Trials: Building on ICARUS-ED Experience
dailyEditorial.takeaway: Read this as sepsis research-methods guidance, not bedside albumin advocacy. The immediate lesson is that better early-intervention trials are feasible if the workflow is built properly.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This piece is really about trial design rather than albumin practice.
dailyEditorial.summary: This piece is really about trial design rather than albumin practice. Its value is in showing that early ED sepsis trials can recruit in real time, maintain protocol fidelity and still cope with diagnostic uncertainty.

dailyEditorial.title: Evaluating the TWIST score and point-of-care ultrasound for paediatric testicular torsion
dailyEditorial.takeaway: Use TWIST and POCUS to structure urgency, not to dismiss a child with persistent torsion concern. High clinical suspicion still beats a reassuring score when the story or exam feels wrong.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This is a practical paediatric diagnostic paper with reassuring numbers and an important warning attached.
dailyEditorial.summary: This is a practical paediatric diagnostic paper with reassuring numbers and an important warning attached. TWIST plus bedside ultrasound performed by emergency clinicians showed high accuracy, but the false-negative rate was still not trivial enough to let the score serve as a rule-out device.

dailyEditorial.title: Systematic Review of Pediatric Ketamine in Emergency Department Procedural Sedation: Frequency and Predictors of Adverse Events
dailyEditorial.takeaway: Keep using ketamine thoughtfully, but lean on better pooled safety data when discussing risk and planning monitoring. Be cautious about overvaluing any predictor that has not been consistently supported across studies.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: Ketamine remains a paediatric ED workhorse, so a large adverse-event review is inherently useful even without a dramatic new warning.
dailyEditorial.summary: Ketamine remains a paediatric ED workhorse, so a large adverse-event review is inherently useful even without a dramatic new warning. The likely gain is better pooled safety estimates and a cleaner sense of which proposed risk factors deserve attention versus repetition.

dailyEditorial.title: Understanding Patients with Mechanical Circulatory Support for Cardiogenic Shock: The Role of Preceding Cardiac Arrest
dailyEditorial.takeaway: Do not treat preceding OHCA as an automatic reason to withhold mechanical circulatory support evaluation. The better question is whether the broader physiology and trajectory make support worthwhile.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This cardiogenic shock paper is helpful because it separates preceding OHCA from the reflex assumption of futility.
dailyEditorial.summary: This cardiogenic shock paper is helpful because it separates preceding OHCA from the reflex assumption of futility. Similar 30-day mortality with different predictors of death suggests these patients should not be collapsed into one simple narrative.
